title: Breakfast burrito
subtitle: Scrambled eggs, sausage, and cheese in a flour tortilla
excerpt: A large flour tortilla wrapped tightly around scrambled eggs, crumbled pork sausage, cheddar, and salsa. The filling is made in one pan, spooned onto the warm tortilla, and rolled into something portable and substantial. Good at any time of day.
sourceNotes: The breakfast burrito as a distinct category (a flour tortilla wrapped around eggs, meat, and cheese) emerged in the American Southwest in the latter half of the twentieth century, building on the longer tradition of the filled burrito in Mexican and Tex-Mex cooking. It became a mainstream American fast-food and home-cooking item from the 1980s onward.

recipe.freezeNotes: Wrap individually in foil and freeze for up to 2 months. Reheat from frozen in a 180°C oven for 20 minutes, or unwrap and microwave for 2 to 3 minutes.

content.text: The key to a burrito that stays together is the roll. The tortilla must be warm before you fill it — a cold tortilla cracks and tears when folded. Thirty seconds in a dry pan or wrapped briefly in a damp cloth and microwaved is enough. Spoon the filling onto the lower third of the tortilla, leaving a border on each side, fold in the sides, then roll forward tightly from the bottom. Set it seam-side down while you make the second one.

Keep the scrambled eggs on the wet side — slightly underdone when they leave the pan, because they will continue cooking as you assemble. Overcooked scrambled eggs turn rubbery inside the burrito. The sausage should be cooked through and slightly crisp at the edges before you add the eggs.
Cook the crumbled sausage meat in the frying pan over medium-high heat for 4 to 5 minutes, breaking it up with a spatula, until browned and cooked through. Transfer to a plate and wipe the pan.

Whisk the {{eggs}} eggs with a pinch of salt and black pepper. Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in the pan over medium-low heat. Add the eggs and cook slowly, stirring with a spatula in broad strokes, for 2 to 3 minutes. Remove from the heat while they are still slightly underdone and glossy.

Warm each {{tortilla-wrap}} tortilla in a dry pan for 20 to 30 seconds per side until pliable. Lay flat on a work surface. Spoon half the scrambled eggs, half the sausage, half the {{cheddar}} of grated cheddar, and {{salsa}} of salsa onto the lower third of each tortilla, leaving a 3 cm border on each side. Add a spoonful of soured cream if using. Fold in the sides, then roll tightly from the bottom. Set seam-side down and serve immediately.

items.fix: Warm the tortilla until it is fully pliable before filling; a cold tortilla will always crack at the fold

items.symptom: Burrito falls apart when picked up
items.cause: Too much filling, or sides not folded in before rolling
items.fix: Keep the filling to the lower third and fold in the sides firmly before rolling forward

items.symptom: Eggs are rubbery inside the burrito
items.cause: Eggs fully cooked before wrapping; residual heat finishes them inside
items.fix: Pull the eggs from the heat while still slightly underdone and glossy; they will set fully in the burrito

items.symptom: Filling is watery and soaks through the tortilla
items.cause: Salsa too liquid, or eggs released water from overcooking
items.fix: Use a thick salsa rather than a watery one; drain briefly before spooning

content.text: The breakfast burrito is a distinctly American invention built on the Mexican flour tortilla, which itself came into wide American use through the cooking traditions of the Southwest and California. It emerged as a named dish in the latter half of the twentieth century and became one of the most widespread American fast-food breakfast items from the 1980s onward — Fast-food chains and dozens of regional chains all serve versions of it. The home version is better, and the appeal is the same: a complete breakfast that stays warm, travels well, and requires only one pan.
